1: Psalm 79 is a national lament almost certainly composed in response to the destruction of Jerusalem — the most likely context being the Babylonian conquest of 586 BCE under Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kgs 25; Jer 39; 52; Lam 1-5), though some scholars date it to the Maccabean period or a prior Assyrian assault. The Asaph superscription links it to the Asaph collection (Pss 50, 73-83), suggesting either authentic Asaph composition or a later text attributed to the Asaphite tradition. "The nations have invaded your inheritance (naḥălātĕkā)" — the land of Canaan is specifically YHWH's inheritance (Deuteronomy 32:9; Psalms 74:2; Psalms 78:71); pagan armies invading it is an invasion of divine property. "Defiled your holy temple (ṭimmĕʾû ʾeṯ-hêkal qodšĕkā)" — ṭāmēʾ (to defile, to make ritually impure) is the cultic term for the violation of sacred space by prohibited contact. The Babylonians brought their cultic objects into the temple (Daniel 1:2) and stripped its sacred vessels — all acts of defilement.
2: "They have given the corpses of your servants as food to the birds of the heavens, the flesh of your godly ones (ḥăsîdêkā) to the beasts of the earth" — the unburied dead as both literal and covenant-theological catastrophe. Deuteronomy 28:26 includes "your carcasses will be food for all the birds of the sky and the wild animals, and there will be no one to frighten them away" in the covenant curse list. The psalmist recognizes the destruction as the enactment of the Deuteronomic curses (Deuteronomy 28:15-68) that Moses warned would result from covenant unfaithfulness. Ḥăsîdêkā (your godly ones, your faithful ones) — from ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty); those who embodied the covenant loyalty YHWH requires are the very ones whose bodies are unburied.
3: "They have poured out their blood like water (šāpĕḵû dāmām kammayim) all around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury the dead (wĕʾên qôbēr)" — blood poured like water conveys both the quantity of killing and the utter disregard for human life. In OT law, blood demanded burial or covering (Genesis 37:26; Ezekiel 24:7-8; Job 16:18); blood poured and left uncovered "cries out" for justice (Genesis 4:10; Habakkuk 2:11-12). "No one to bury" — proper burial was the most basic social obligation in the ancient world (Gen 23; Tobit; 2 Samuel 21:12-14). Unburied corpses represented the complete breakdown of civilization and the ultimate dishonor for the dead. In ANE thought, unburied shades could not rest in the underworld and wandered in misery.
4: "We have become a reproach (ḥerpāh) to our neighbors, a mockery (laʿag) and derision (qeles) to those around us" — the social humiliation of conquest in an honor-shame culture. Ḥerpāh (reproach, disgrace, shame) — the most comprehensive social-shame term in the OT; used in Psalms 22:6; Psalms 44:13-16; Psalms 69:7-12, 19-20; 74:22 for Israel's shame before the nations. "Mockery (laʿag) and derision (qeles)" — the neighboring nations celebrated Jerusalem's fall (Lamentations records this: "All who pass by clap their hands at you; they scoff and shake their heads," Lamentations 2:15; Ezekiel 25:3 — the Ammonites said "Aha!" at the destruction of the sanctuary). The defeat of a nation's army proved the defeat of its god in the ancient world — Jerusalem's fall was read as YHWH's defeat.
5: "How long (ʿad-māh), O LORD? Will you be angry forever (lānĕṣaḥ)? Will your jealousy (qinʾātĕkā) burn like fire?" — the temporal challenge of the lament (also Psalms 6:3; Psalms 13:1-2; Psalms 35:17; Psalms 74:10; Psalms 80:4; Psalms 89:46; Psalms 90:13; Psalms 94:3; Habakkuk 1:2). "Forever (lānĕṣaḥ)" — the covenant promise of YHWH's covenant was eternal (bĕrît ʿôlām); the lament asks whether divine anger can also be eternal, which would contradict the covenant. "Jealousy (qinʾāh) burn like fire" — the same divine jealousy that destroyed Israel (Psalms 78:58) is now the wrath being endured; the psalmist uses the covenant term to argue that the jealousy that brought punishment must eventually burn itself out in renewed covenant loyalty to Israel.
6: "Pour out your wrath (šĕpōk ḥămaṯĕkā) on the nations that do not know you, and on the kingdoms that do not call on your name!" — the redirection petition: the divine wrath currently being experienced by Israel should be redirected to the Gentile nations who are actually YHWH's enemies. Jeremiah 10:25 is nearly verbatim: "Pour out your wrath on the nations that do not acknowledge you, on the peoples who do not call on your name." The question is whether Jeremiah 10:25 quotes Psalms 79:6 or both draw on a common liturgical tradition — either way, the prayer was used in both contexts. "Nations that do not know you (ʾăšer lōʾ yĕdāʿûkā)" — the covenant-knowing of YHWH (Jeremiah 31:34; John 17:3) versus the Gentile ignorance; not intellectual knowledge but covenant relationship.
7: "For they have devoured Jacob (kî ʾākal ʾeṯ-yaʿăqōb) and laid waste (wĕʾeṯ-nāwēhû hĕšammû) his homeland" — the "devoured" language frames the Babylonian conquest in predator-prey terms: Israel as the eaten prey. Nāweh (pasture, homeland, lovely abode) — from the same root as the beautiful dwelling place; this is the promised land as home, the "pleasant land" (ʾereṣ ḥemdāh) that was Israel's covenant inheritance. "Laid waste (šāmam)" — the desolation vocabulary that runs through Lamentations (Lamentations 1:4; Psalms 3:11) and the prophets' descriptions of the post-destruction landscape.
8: "Do not hold our former iniquities against us (ʾal-tizkār-lānû ʿăwōnōṯ rišōnîm); let your compassion (raḥămêkā) come quickly to meet us, for we are utterly brought low (dallônû meʾōd)" — the penitential petition acknowledges that the destruction was deserved (the "former iniquities" of the covenant violations), but asks for mercy rather than strict justice. Zakar (to remember, to call to mind, to hold to account) — the petition is that YHWH not "remember" the sins in the sense of holding them against the community. "Come quickly to meet us (yĕqaddĕmûnû raḥămêkā)" — let compassion precede (qādam, to meet, to come before, to be in front of) any further judgment. "Utterly brought low (dāal meʾōd)" — dāal (to be low, weak, thin, powerless) — the physical and social depletion of the destroyed community.
9: "Help us, O God of our salvation (ʾĕlōhê yišʿēnû), for the glory of your name (lĕmaʿan kĕbôd-šĕmĕkā); deliver us and forgive our sins (kapper ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾṯênû), for your name's sake!" — the two appeals: the glory of the divine name and the atoning forgiveness. The "for your name's sake" appeal (also Psalms 23:3; Psalms 25:11; Psalms 31:3; Psalms 106:8; Jeremiah 14:7, 21; Ezekiel 20:9, 14, 22, 44; Daniel 9:19) grounds the petition not in Israel's merit but in YHWH's own reputation and covenant identity. Kāpar (to atone, to cover, to wipe away guilt) — the cultic atonement term here used in direct petition: the psalmist asks YHWH to perform the atonement without the cultic machinery (the temple being destroyed). This anticipates the theology of forgiveness apart from temple sacrifice developed in the exile (cf. Dan 9; Neh 9).
10: "Why should the nations say, 'Where is their God?' (ʾayyēh ʾĕlōhêhem)" — the honor-shame challenge of the nations is the strongest argument available to the psalmist. If YHWH allows Jerusalem to be destroyed without response, the nations will conclude that YHWH is either absent, defeated, or indifferent. This exact taunt appears in Psalms 42:3, 10; Psalms 115:2; Joel 2:17; Micah 7:10 — it was a recognizable formula of national humiliation in the ANE. "Before our eyes, let the avenging of your servants' shed blood be known among the nations" — divine justice performed publicly, in the nations' sight, is the solution to the honor-shame problem: YHWH's reputation is restored when his justice becomes visible.
11: "Let the groaning of the prisoner (ʾanqaṯ āsîr) come before you; according to your great power (gōdel zĕrôʿĕkā), preserve those condemned to die (bĕnê ṯĕmûṯāh)!" — the prisoners are those taken into Babylonian exile or imprisoned after the conquest. "Condemned to die (bĕnê ṯĕmûṯāh)" — literally "children of death," those for whom death seems the inevitable outcome. The petition for divine power to preserve those who seem beyond help echoes Psalms 22:20-21 (rescue my life from the sword) and anticipates the dry-bones vision of Ezek 37 — the exiles as those already dead whom YHWH will resurrect into new life. "Your great power (gōdel zĕrôʿĕkā)" — the arm/strength of YHWH as the basis of the petition: the same arm that divided the sea (Psalms 77:15; Isaiah 51:9-10) can divide the circumstances of the prisoner.
12: "Return to our neighbors sevenfold into their bosom (šibʿāṯayim ʾel-ḥêqām) the reproach (ḥerpāh) with which they have taunted you, O Lord" — the sevenfold retribution formula is the maximum intensity retributive petition in the OT. Šibʿāṯayim (sevenfold) — the ultimate multiplier of judgment: Genesis 4:15 (Cain protected by sevenfold vengeance); Leviticus 26:18, 21, 24, 28 (sevenfold covenant curses); Proverbs 6:31 (the thief must restore sevenfold); Matthew 18:22 (forgive seventy-seven times — the inverse of sevenfold vengeance). "Into their bosom (ḥêq)" — the bosom as the place where things are stored and carried (Ruth 4:16; Psalms 74:11; Isaiah 40:11 — the shepherd carrying lambs in his bosom); sevenfold shame returned into the bosom of the mockers. The petition invokes poetic justice: what they delivered to Israel (shame, v4) returns to them multiplied.
13: "Then we your people, the sheep of your pasture (ṣōʾn marʿîṯĕkā), will give you thanks forever (lĕʿôlām); from generation to generation (lĕdōr wādōr) we will recount (nĕsappēr) your praise" — the psalm closes with the vow of endless, intergenerational praise — which is exactly what Psalm 78 opened with as its program (v4: "we will tell the next generation of the LORD's praiseworthy deeds"). Psalm 79 begins with the destruction of what Psalm 78 celebrated (the temple, Zion, the Davidic heritage) and ends by recommitting to the intergenerational recitation program. "The sheep of your pasture" echoes Psalms 74:1; Psalms 78:52; Psalms 95:7; Psalms 100:3 — the covenant people as YHWH's dependent flock, the shepherd-king David's typological heritage applied to the entire community. The psalm closes in hope: even from the ruins, the thanksgiving tradition will be renewed.
